comment: Ordered curriculum. Tier 1 (Control Mastery, lessons 1-12, wording locked except the 'fader-bring-in' enforcement fix) plus Tier 2 (Timing, section 'Timing'). Each lesson has a stable string 'id' (engine walks by index, but ids survive reordering). CONTROL lessons target a control id from control-map.json; 'hear' is the audible result on completion; 'done' tightens the check ({minValue}/{maxValue}, or {stages:[...]} for an in-order sequence); a lesson may instead define 'steps' (each with its own instruction/control/done, optional 'also' co-spotlight ids); optional 'resetControls' returns listed controls to centre on entry. TIMING lessons set 'type':'timing' with a pre-aligned 'track' {src,deck,bpm,offset} and steps carrying a 'mode': 'tap' (count 'taps' on-beat), 'demo' ('highlight':'bar'|'phrase'), or 'evaluate' (grade the learner's action vs the nearest boundary: 'target':'bar'|'phrase8'|'phrase', 'attempts', 'leadInBeats'; success window is the PHRASE_TIMING_WINDOW_MS constant in index.html). Timing steps have no control id.

lessons.title: Start the music
lessons.instruction: Press the glowing PLAY button on the left deck.
lessons.hear: Your track starts. Press it again — it stops. This is the only button that matters until you know what the others do.

lessons.title: Set the cue point
lessons.instruction: With the track paused, press CUE.
lessons.hear: The track jumps back to where you set your cue. Hold CUE to preview from there; release and it snaps back.

lessons.title: The channel fader (your volume)
lessons.instruction: With the track playing, pull the Channel 1 fader all the way down, then push it back up.
lessons.hear: Your track disappears and comes back. This fader is the single most important control on the mixer — it decides whether a deck is heard at all.

lessons.title: HI: the crisp stuff
lessons.instruction: Turn the HI knob on Channel 1 all the way down (counter-clockwise).
lessons.hear: The hi-hats and cymbals vanish. The track gets dull and muffled. Turn it back to 12 o'clock — they come back.

lessons.title: LOW: the kick and bass
lessons.instruction: Turn the LOW knob on Channel 1 all the way down.
lessons.hear: The kick drum and bassline drop out. The track gets thin and hollow. This is called "killing the bass" — it's the most-used move in all of DJing.

lessons.title: MID: everything else
lessons.instruction: Turn the MID knob on Channel 1 all the way down.
lessons.hear: Vocals and melody hollow out. MIDs are where most of the music actually lives — that's why cutting them sounds so strange.

lessons.title: Tempo: the speed
lessons.instruction: Slide the left TEMPO fader up, then down.
lessons.hear: The track speeds up and slows down. Notice the pitch shifts too — that's normal. This fader exists for exactly one reason: making two songs run at the same speed.

lessons.title: The crossfader
lessons.instruction: Slide the crossfader fully left, then fully right.
lessons.hear: With only Deck 1 playing, your track vanishes when the crossfader goes right — because the crossfader chooses which deck reaches the speakers.

lessons.title: Deck 2 is the same deck
lessons.instruction: Load a track to the right deck and press its PLAY button.
lessons.hear: Both tracks playing at once. It sounds like a mess — that's the point. Everything you just learned on the left exists identically on the right.

lessons.title: Cue: hear it before they do
lessons.instruction: Pull the Channel 2 fader down, then press the CUE button on Channel 2 and put on headphones.

steps.instruction: First, pull the Channel 2 fader all the way down.
Now press the CUE button on Channel 2 and put on headphones.

lessons.hear: Deck 2 in your headphones only. The room still hears Deck 1. This is the DJ's secret: you're listening to the future.

lessons.title: Bring it in with the fader
lessons.instruction: With Deck 2 playing and cued in your headphones, pull the Channel 2 fader all the way down, then slowly raise it to bring the track in.
lessons.hear: The second track enters the mix. Too loud, too sudden — it clashes. You just did your first (bad) transition. Now we fix it.

lessons.title: Your first real transition (bass swap)

steps.instruction: Raise the Deck 2 fader all the way.
Now slowly turn Deck 1's LOW down as you turn Deck 2's LOW up.

lessons.hear: The tracks layer without fighting. Two basslines at once is mud — that's why you kill one. As you swap the LOWs, the energy hands off from one track to the other. That's a real DJ transition.

lessons.title: Hear the beat
lessons.instruction: Here's a track whose grid is already lined up for you. Let's learn what those lines mean — by ear.

steps.instruction: Tap along to the beat: hit the SPACEBAR (or the big TAP button) each time the playhead crosses a beat line.
steps.hear: That steady pulse is the beat — the heartbeat of the track.

steps.instruction: Four beats make a BAR. Watch one bar light up as it plays, then press Continue.
steps.hear: Bars are how DJs count: "1, 2, 3, 4" is one bar.

steps.instruction: Every 32 bars the music changes — that's a PHRASE, marked by the thick red line. Listen for the change as the playhead reaches it. This is where DJs bring in the next track.
steps.hear: Beat → bar → phrase. That hierarchy is the map every DJ mixes to.

lessons.hear: You've got the skeleton of every track: beat, bar, phrase. Next, you'll start using it.

lessons.title: Mix on the phrase
lessons.instruction: Now use the grid. As the track reaches the red phrase line, make your move — right on the line.

steps.instruction: Watch the countdown to the red phrase line. On the hit, press MIX (or the spacebar), or slam the crossfader across. I'll tell you how close you were — early, late, or right on.
steps.hear: Right on the line is where the next track drops.

lessons.hear: That's the whole game: land your move on the phrase. Tighten that up and you're mixing like a DJ.
